title_en: How KPop Demon Hunters Star EJAE Topped the Charts
title_zh: 《Golden》背后主唱编曲人 EJAE 如何登顶榜单

paragraphs.en: EJAE, the voice and the writing talent behind “Golden,” has gone platinum.
paragraphs.zh: 为《Golden》提供声线与创作才华的 EJAE，如今已获得白金认证。

paragraphs.en: The night before our interview, the 33-year-old singer-songwriter found out that record sales from the KPop Demon Hunters soundtrack had surged past a million units. Jimmy Fallon, of all people, delivered the news alongside a glimmering framed record when she was appearing on The Tonight Show with Audrey Nuna and Rei Ami for the first full live performance of “Golden.” Together the trio make up the singing voices of girl group Huntr/x in Netflix’s animated musical turned bona fide phenomenon. If you have a kid, you probably don’t need a refresher, but the movie follows Huntr/x’s Rumi, Mira, and Zoey as they juggle being astronomically famous while moonlighting as demon hunters (EJAE sings as the purple-haired Rumi). Their singing voices double as their weapons, shielding their fans from the soul-sucking minions of underworld ruler Gwi-Ma.
paragraphs.zh: 在我们采访的前一晚，这位 33 岁的歌手兼词曲作者得知《KPop恶魔猎人》原声带唱片销量已突破一百万张。汤米·法隆（Jimmy Fallon）竟然在她与 Audrey Nuna 和 Rei Ami 一起首次完整现场演唱《Golden》时，拿着闪亮的镶框唱片一起宣布了这个消息。三人组成了 Netflix 动画音乐片《Huntr/x》里的“三重唱”男孩团体，在该片中这三位角色的歌唱部分由她们配音。若你有孩子，可能不必再提醒：这部动画讲述了“Huntr/x”中的 Rumi、Mira 和 Zoey 三人如何在拥有天文数字般的人气之余，兼职去当恶魔猎人（EJAE 为紫发的 Rumi 配音）。她们的歌声兼作武器，保护粉丝免受冥界统治者 Gwi-Ma 及其吸血夺魂小队的侵害。

paragraphs.en: That Fallon appearance, and the Saturday Night Live appearance that predated it, might have been the first times that American audiences actually saw (and heard) the human being behind that inescapable song. The adventure fantasy—the directorial debut of Korean-Canadian filmmaker Maggie Kang, who codirected it with Chris Appelhans—is Netflix’s most popular film of all time, with over 400 million views. When Netflix released a sing-along version in theaters, it brought in nearly $20 million, dominating the box office. The soundtrack is one of the highest charting of the year, with more than 7 billion streams. “Golden,” which EJAE partially came up with on a cab ride to the dentist, was number 1 on Billboard’s Hot 100 for eight weeks (as of this writing, it’s only bested by Taylor Swift but remains in the top 10). The promotional tour for the film, which debuted in June, seems to have never ended.
paragraphs.zh: 那次法隆的出镜，以及其后不久的《周六夜现场》亮相，或许是美国观众第一次真正看到并听见这个“上头”歌曲背后真实的人。这个奇幻冒险片是韩裔加拿大导演 Maggie Kang 的导演处女作，她与 Chris Appelhans 共同执导，如今成为 Netflix 历史上最受欢迎的电影之一，累计观看量超过 4 亿次。当 Netflix 在影院推出卡拉 OK 版本时，票房几乎拿下 2,000 万美元，成为票房主导。该片原声带成为今年榜单成绩最亮眼之一，流媒体播放量超过 70 亿次。EJAE 在出租车上“灵感突发”完成部分词曲的《Golden》，在 Billboard《百强单曲榜》上连任 8 周冠军（截至发稿时仅次于泰勒·斯威夫特，但仍保持前十）。这部于六月上映的电影宣传周边似乎一直没有结束。

paragraphs.en: Five-foot-nine and wearing heels, EJAE towers over me when we meet at Netflix’s Manhattan offices. She’s wearing her hair in three braids, reminiscent of Rumi’s iconic hairstyle. Despite looking every bit the part of a K-pop star, EJAE doesn’t consider herself famous. Nor does she particularly want to be, which is somewhat unexpected, considering she was a trainee at famed K-pop label SM Entertainment for more than a decade.
paragraphs.zh: 与我在 Netflix 位于曼哈顿的办公室见面时，EJAE 身高 5 英尺 9 英寸，踩着高跟鞋，整个人在我面前很突出。她把头发梳成三股辫，像极了 Rumi 的标志性发型。尽管外表完全像一名 K-pop 明星，她却并不认为自己是“出名的人”。考虑到她曾在知名 K-pop 唱片公司 SM Entertainment 练习生身份待了十多年，这一点反而有些出乎预料；她也并不特别想出名。

paragraphs.en: Not unlike American boy-band-forming talent machines of the ’90s, K-pop trainee programs start teaching future stars as children. Trainees keep intense schedules, where they sing, dance, rap, and manage their physical appearances and personas in the hopes of debuting into a K-pop group and becoming an idol. For EJAE, that moment never came. She was passed over time and time again until she was dropped by the label at 23, which she describes as “grandma age for the K-pop industry.” Her voice and her personality, she felt, were better suited for the behind-the-scenes role of songwriting. A decade on, she’s been thrust into the limelight in perhaps the most meta way imaginable—as the voice of a K-pop star that people can’t seem to get enough of. (She has also just released a single of her own music.) She tells WIRED how she’s navigating her newfound success, what it’s like being in the grueling K-pop machine, and the key to writing an absolute banger.
paragraphs.zh: 这与1990年代美国男孩乐队培养工厂并无太大不同：K-pop练习生制度从孩子时期就开始训练未来的明星。练习生们日程非常紧张，唱歌、跳舞、说唱，还要管理外形和个人形象，目标是出道成为K-pop组合的一员并成为偶像。对于EJAE来说，这个瞬间从未到来。她一次次被“PASS”，直到23岁被公司淘汰，她把这称作“在K-pop行业里是奶奶年龄”。她觉得自己的嗓音和性格更适合幕后写歌。十年后，她却以一种几乎最“元”也最戏剧化的方式进入公众视野——成为人们听都听不腻的K-pop偶像的嗓音。（她也刚发布了自己的单曲。）她在接受《WIRED》采访时谈到自己如何应对新近到来的成功、在高强度的K-pop流水线体系里是什么体验，以及如何写出一首真正上头的神曲。

paragraphs.en: This interview has been edited and condensed for clarity.
paragraphs.zh: 为便于阅读和理解，这篇采访经过了编辑和删减。

paragraphs.en: MANISHA KRISHNAN: How’s it going? Were you just doing a photo shoot upstairs?
paragraphs.zh: MANISHA KRISHNAN：最近怎么样？你刚在楼上拍照吗？

paragraphs.en: EJAE: Oh yeah. I’ve been doing a lot. We’ve been up since like 5 am.
paragraphs.zh: EJAE：是的。最近工作特别多。我们从早上五点左右就开始忙了。

paragraphs.en: You must be so tired.
paragraphs.zh: 你一定累坏了吧。

paragraphs.en: I’m so tired. Fallon yesterday and SNL.
paragraphs.zh: 我真的很累。昨天还做了理发（或染发）排练《Fallon》加《周六夜现场》.

paragraphs.en: I was getting my nails done yesterday and “Golden” came on. It’s been on the Billboard Hot 100 for weeks. What’s it like knowing that every kid in America knows the words to your song?
paragraphs.zh: 我昨天去做指甲，正好放着《Golden》。它已经在《公告牌百强单曲榜》上停留了好几个星期。知道美国每个孩子都能唱出你这首歌的歌词是什么感受？

paragraphs.en: Oh my God, it’s like a dream come true as a songwriter. Kids are so blunt—so the fact that they love it, it’s like, I feel real proud of myself.
paragraphs.zh: 天啊，作为词曲作者来说，这就像梦想成真。孩子们很直接，所以他们爱上这首歌这件事让我真的很自豪。

paragraphs.en: The toughest demographic.
paragraphs.zh: 最难打动的人群。

paragraphs.en: It is hard to get kids’ stamp of approval, so I feel like a cool person.
paragraphs.zh: 要赢得孩子们的认可很难，所以我觉得自己很酷。

paragraphs.en: The movie came out in June. It’s become Netflix’s most-watched project. There’s Oscar buzz. You’ve been doing the late-night circuit. What has been the most dramatic part of all of this for you?
paragraphs.zh: 这部电影于6月上映。它已成为Netflix观看人数最多的项目。现在大家都在谈论奥斯卡。你一直在晚间脱口秀节目中亮相。对你来说，这一切最戏剧化的部分是哪一段？

paragraphs.en: They’re all quite dramatic and crazy and so surreal. But yeah, I mean obviously SNL and Fallon were just definitely peak, especially Fallon, because he gave us the plaque, the platinum plaque.
paragraphs.zh: 它们都非常戏剧化、疯狂，而且非常超现实。但说实话，SNL和福隆（Fallon）绝对是最高潮的，尤其是福隆，因为他给了我们那块牌匾——白金奖牌。

paragraphs.en: He really surprised us. I was genuinely so stunned. We were all crying.
paragraphs.zh: 他真把我们惊到了。我当时是真的震惊到说不出话。我们都哭了。

paragraphs.en: When you were on SNL, were you nervous to sing?
paragraphs.zh: 你在《周六夜现场》登场时，会紧张唱歌吗？

paragraphs.en: I’m always nervous to sing. I’m nervous in the studio to sing, so the fact that I’m performing it is a huge jump for me. My comfort zone and usually where I feel the best singing is my own little corner in my house in Brooklyn.
paragraphs.zh: 我唱歌时总是很紧张。进录音棚唱歌时也是，所以我能上台演唱对我来说是一个很大的跨越。我的舒适区通常也是我唱得最好的地方，就是我在布鲁克林家里的小小一角。

paragraphs.en: What is your relationship with fame right now? Do you consider yourself famous?
paragraphs.zh: 你现在和名气是什么关系？你会觉得自己出名吗？

paragraphs.en: No. I consider the song famous, which I love. Fame is a tricky thing. I was a K-pop trainee myself, and my grandpa’s an actor, so I’ve seen fame from different angles, and it’s very beautiful in the front, but it can get very, very dark. I feel like fame is definitely not for the faint of heart, and so I’m trying to navigate through it.
paragraphs.zh: 不是。我觉得这首歌很有名，也很喜欢。名气这东西很微妙。我自己也当过K-pop练习生，而且我爷爷是演员，所以我从不同角度见过名气。它在前台看起来很美，但也可能非常、非常阴暗。我感觉名气绝对不是给胆小人准备的，所以我正在努力在其中找到方向。

paragraphs.en: I was a trainee and then I got dropped and I wasn’t singing. But to be honest, that was an intentional choice. I was like, being an artist and a singer doesn’t fit my personality. I kind of let that go. So suddenly getting all this attention is very new. I can’t quite say it’s natural for me. I love being behind the scenes. But I’m trying to get used to it. I don’t know if I should get used to it, but yeah, it’s very fascinating, and I’m always grateful.
paragraphs.zh: 我曾经是练习生，后来被解约，之前也没在歌唱。说实话，那其实是我有意的选择。我想，成为一名艺术家和歌手并不太符合我的性格。我就这样放手了。结果突然得到这么多关注对我来说非常新鲜。我不能说这对我来说很自然。我喜欢在幕后工作。可我也在努力适应。我不知道自己到底该不该适应，但确实很吸引人，而且我一直心存感激。

paragraphs.en: In light of everything that’s going on right now, have you sort of changed your mind about whether or not you want to be a performing artist?
paragraphs.zh: 考虑到现在正在发生的一切，你有没有因此改变过是否想当一名表演艺术家的想法？

paragraphs.en: Songwriting has always been my therapy, and it’s kind of where I get away from everything. So I’m like, maybe I can combine that, and maybe being a songwriter is my artistry. As an artist, I don’t really want to have my personal things be in the lyrics. I want the lyrics and the songs to be quite universal for everyone to connect to, like how “Golden” did. So I kind of want my songs to be pop stars, not necessarily me.
paragraphs.zh: 作词写歌一直是我的疗愈方式，也是我从一切压力中逃离的地方。所以我在想，也许我可以把二者结合起来，而也许一名词曲创作者就是我的创作身份。作为一个艺术家，我并不想把自己的私事写进歌词。我希望歌词和歌曲都尽量是普遍的，任何人都能共鸣，像《Golden》那样。所以我有点希望我的歌曲是流行佳作，而不一定是“我”本人的故事。

paragraphs.en: Can you tell me a little bit of the story of “Golden,” and did you know it was going to be a hit right off the bat?
paragraphs.zh: 你能讲讲《Golden》的一点故事吗？你当时知道它一出场就会大火吗？

paragraphs.en: I mean, in our little circle, yes, it was a hit but definitely not this caliber at all. The melody came quite instantly. It was on my way to the dentist.
paragraphs.zh: 我的意思是，在我们这一小圈子里，确实算是个热门，但绝对没有想象中会达到这种级别。旋律来得非常快。那时候我正赶去看牙医。

paragraphs.en: Getting a gold filling, by the way. So the melody came really fast, but when we finished it, I had this session with Mark [Sonnenblick], my cowriter, right after, and Mark was so excited. I was like, “I have this melody.” He heard it and was like, “Oh my God, this is amazing.” So when we finished the whole song and the lyrics, we just looked at ourselves and we’re like, “Wow, I think this is a smash.”
paragraphs.zh: 顺便说一下，是去补一个金属充填。旋律一下子就冒出来了。等我们把它做完后，我紧接着和我的共同创作者马克·索嫩布里克（Mark Sonnenblick）开了一次会，马克当时非常兴奋。我说：“我有这个旋律了。”他听后就说：“天哪，这太棒了。”于是当我们把整首歌和歌词都完成时，我们只是互相看着，心里想：“哇，我觉得这会是个大热。”

paragraphs.en: Is there a formula to making a K-pop hit or an algorithm to it, or is it just different every time?
paragraphs.zh: 做出一首K-pop热门歌有公式吗，或者说有算法吗，还是每次都完全不同？

paragraphs.en: For a K-pop hit, a US hit, and a global hit, it’s all quite different. I think for a K-pop hit, being hooky is definitely important. Global hit, I’ve noticed, is a concept that everyone can say—everyone knows what “Golden” means. Everyone has a feeling of wanting hope in their life, especially right now at this time. In the US that’s also important too. So I think universally, when the concept is really good and it’s easy to say, and when there’s a good melody and a great message, you have a hit.
paragraphs.zh: 要做到K-pop爆款、美国爆款和全球爆款，做法其实各有不同。我觉得对于K-pop爆款来说，旋律容易让人上瘾绝对很重要。至于全球爆款，我发现这是一个每个人都能说清楚的概念——每个人都明白“Golden”意味着什么。每个人内心都有一种想要希望的渴望，尤其是在当下。到了美国也是一样的。 所以我觉得，只要主题非常好、说起来顺口，再加上优美的旋律和很有力量的讯息，你就有可能做出一首热曲。

paragraphs.en: Do you think the intense political atmosphere right now is part of why this movie and this song have taken off?
paragraphs.zh: 你觉得当下紧张的政治氛围是这部电影和这首歌走红的原因之一吗？

paragraphs.en: I think so. A friend of mine told me that his friend has kids, and KPop Demon Hunters is bringing light to their kid that they can hold onto and just kind of focus on that and kind of ignore the dark times right now. It brings them hope. It has a really beautiful message, makes them want to love all of themselves, all their flaws and all their good parts too.
paragraphs.zh: 我觉得是这样的。有个朋友跟我说，他的一个朋友有孩子，而《KPop Demon Hunters》给这些孩子带来了光亮，他们可以抓住这份力量，集中注意力在它上面，从而暂时把眼前的黑暗时刻放在一边。它给了他们希望。它有一个非常美好的信息，让他们想要去爱自己——既包括自己的缺点，也包括自己的优点。

paragraphs.en: I want to go back to you being a trainee for SM Entertainment. How old were you when that happened? Can you tell me a little bit about that world?
paragraphs.zh: 我想回到你在SM Entertainment当练习生的经历。那时你多大了？能不能和我说说那个世界？

paragraphs.en: I had a dream of wanting to become a singer when I was 3, 4, and then I started auditioning around 10 or 11. I got in and started training quite immediately. You learn how to dance, sing, jazz dance, rap, Chinese, Japanese, the whole thing. All day, literally. So if we have school, right after school we go immediately to train. Every couple months there would be a showcase of what you’ve learned. Every Monday, all the trainees would join in one room, and we would go in front of the camera and sing and do our dance moves. It’s like a weekly checkup. So that was quite something, and we would have to weigh ourselves every week too. So it was intense. I’m not going to lie.
paragraphs.zh: 我三四岁时就有过想当歌手的梦想，后来在十岁或十一岁开始了试镜。通过之后，我很快就开始训练。你要学舞蹈、唱歌、爵士舞、说唱、中文、日文，样样都学。整天都在练习，真的整天都在。比如说放学后有课程，我们放学后就立刻去训练。每隔两三个月会有一次展示，展示我们学到的内容。每个星期一，所有练习生都会在一个房间里，站到镜头前唱歌跳舞。这就像一次每周体检。那时真的很紧张，我们还要每周称体重。所以非常高强度，我不骗你。

paragraphs.en: What was the criticism like? Was it harsh? How do you cope with that at a young age?
paragraphs.zh: 当时的批评是怎样的？会不会很严厉？你当时这么小该怎么应对？

paragraphs.en: Damn, I don’t think I knew what coping meant at that age. We just take it literally, especially when you’re 11, when you’re going through puberty is when girls are so sensitive. I feel like when we’re teenagers, we are very sensitive to a lot of things. It wasn’t easy. It’s very competitive, and you’re kind of trying to do this job that’s essentially an adult job, you know what I mean? All I could do was work hard and always try to put my best foot forward. I was always craving a compliment, like, “Oh, you did so good today.” I just wanted something.
paragraphs.zh: 天哪，我那时候根本不知道“应对”是什么意思。我们只会按字面去理解，尤其是你11岁那会，正值青春期，女孩特别敏感。我感觉我们十几岁时对很多事情都很敏感。那很不容易。这个行业很竞争，你在做的其实是一份成年人才能做的工作，你懂吧？我能做的只有更努力，而且一直把自己最好的状态摆出来。我总是很渴望别人的表扬，比如有人说一句“你今天真的很棒”。我只想要那样的一句肯定。

paragraphs.en: Did you get those very often?
paragraphs.zh: 你经常能得到这种表扬吗？

paragraphs.en: Not really. Not much. It’s very objective. Looking at it now as an adult, I understand the reason behind certain things when it comes to marketing or something like that, but at a young age it’s not easy to hear. So I’m glad the fame is coming now, not when I was a kid.
paragraphs.zh: 不，几乎没有。确实不多。它很客观。现在我已经是成年人，再回头看时，我明白有些事情——比如说市场营销之类——背后的原因，但在年轻的时候很难听懂。所以我很高兴这份名声是在我现在来得，而不是我还是个孩子的时候。

paragraphs.en: Looking at where you are now, does it give you any satisfaction, just thinking back on the people who maybe didn’t believe in you or who didn’t give you an opportunity? You can be petty.
paragraphs.zh: 看到你现在的处境，再回想那些可能不相信你、也没给你机会的人，会不会让你觉得有些满足？你可能会有点记恨。

paragraphs.en: Back then I was not at the level that I am now. I couldn’t sing those high notes back then, so I get where they were coming from. I think everything happens for a reason. But I do feel satisfied in the fact that I didn’t give up and I just kept going. And I think the biggest regret I had when I got dropped was I felt like I let down 11-year-old EJAE, who really wanted to become a singer. So in that respect, I hope I made her proud.
paragraphs.zh: 那时的我还没有现在这么强。那时我唱不了那些高音，所以我也能理解他们当时的想法。我认为一切都有原因。但我确实很满足于自己没有放弃，只是一直接着前进。我想我在被取消资格那次最大的遗憾，就是觉得自己让11岁的EJAE失望了，而她是真心想成为一名歌手的。就这点来说，我希望我让她感到骄傲。

paragraphs.en: I was listening to an interview with the film’s codirector Maggie Kang and she was saying how it took her years to even be able to pitch this movie. What does it mean to have this breakthrough moment and for that to be an Asian story and a Korean story?
paragraphs.zh: 我在听电影联合导演Kang Maggie的采访，她说她花了好多年才终于有机会把这部电影拿出去推介。这种“突破时刻”意味着什么？更何况它是一部亚洲故事、也是一部韩国故事。

paragraphs.en: Oh my God, it means the world. It means so much as a Korean American, Asian American woman. And it just feels so cool to be finally portrayed as not the typical portrayal of Asian women—subservient and quiet and passive and always listening. Cause I’m not really like that. I’m quite outspoken, I’m quite blunt. And I just feel like finally there’s a superhero movie about Korean women, and they’re so funny. They’re goofy. They’re not just solely strong and badass, but they eat their food crazy. I do that all the time. We binge on our food.
paragraphs.zh: 天啊，这对我意义重大。作为一位韩裔美国人、亚裔美国女性，它意味太多了。它让我感觉终于不再被描绘成典型的亚洲女性形象——顺从、安静、被动、总是“恭恭敬敬”地听从。因为我其实并不这样。我很直率，也很坦率。我还觉得，终于有一部关于韩裔女性的超级英雄电影，而且她们很有趣，甚至有点傻里傻气。她们不只是强硬和酷，更会拼命吃东西。其实我也是这样，我们总爱把食物吃得很过瘾。

paragraphs.en: What do the next few months look like for you? How are you going to leverage what’s happening now? I’ve seen fans say that they want you to go on tour and have holograms of the characters. Would you do that?
paragraphs.zh: 接下来的几个月对你来说会是怎样？你打算如何利用现在发生的这一切？我看到粉丝说他们希望你去巡演，还想要角色全息影像。你愿意做吗？

paragraphs.en: Who knows? I mean, I’d be down, but it’s not up to me. But yeah, absolutely. I mean, KPop Demon Hunters fans are amazing. They’re so nice. So nice.
paragraphs.zh: 谁知道呢？我的意思是，我当然愿意，但这不完全由我决定。不过，完全可以。我的意思是，KPop Demon Hunters 的粉丝太棒了。他们很友善。非常友善。

paragraphs.en: You sound surprised.
paragraphs.zh: 听起来你有点惊讶。

paragraphs.en: No, no. It can be scary, but they’re so welcoming, so understanding, so encouraging. So hell yeah, I would love to meet them too.
paragraphs.zh: 不是，不是的。和他们在一起可能会有点可怕，但他们真的很热情、很能理解人、很鼓励人。所以，当然了，我也很想去见见他们。

paragraphs.en: I did actually want to ask you about the K-pop fandom, because it seems kind of intimidating.
paragraphs.zh: 我其实真想问你关于 K-pop 应援文化的事，因为看起来确实有点让人不敢接近。

paragraphs.en: It’s intimidating, absolutely. I do appreciate their passion. When I was a K-pop trainee there was a boy band called TVXQ. They were huge at that time. And I still remember, there was a third-floor dance practice room, and to walk up from the first floor we had to pass this glass door. Fans were all over that door, and they would try to open it. Once they had a little bit of a crack open, and whenever female trainees would go up, they would throw trash and call us really bad names. I just remember feeling so scared.
paragraphs.zh: 确实很让人敬畏，完全是。我很欣赏他们的热情。那时我还在当 K-pop 实习生的时候，有一个男团叫 TVXQ，当时他们很红。我还记得三楼有一间舞蹈练习室，从一楼上去时必须经过一扇玻璃门。粉丝们总是聚在那扇门前，试图把它打开。门一旦被他们撬开一点点，只要有女生实习生上楼，他们就会往里扔垃圾，还会骂我们非常难听的话。我只记得那时非常害怕。

paragraphs.en: What do you think that was about?
paragraphs.zh: 你觉得那到底是什么回事？

paragraphs.en: It’s tricky when you’re idolizing someone so much. It can get very dark, fast—and toxic. A lot of the fans are teenagers, and I feel like when you’re a teenager, you’re going through puberty, you feel lots of intense emotions.
paragraphs.zh: 当你对某个人过度偶像化时，情况会变得很复杂。它可能很快变得十分阴暗，而且具有毒性。很多粉丝都是十几岁的青少年，我觉得在青春期，情绪会特别强烈。

paragraphs.en: What is your relationship to ambition right now?
paragraphs.zh: 你现在和“野心”是什么关系？

paragraphs.en: What a cool question. Toxic. I’m kidding.
paragraphs.zh: 这是个很酷的问题。很“toxic”——我开玩笑的。

paragraphs.en: It can be, right? I swear. That’s like “Golden.” “Golden” is a bittersweet song. Technically it sounds like it’s hopeful. It is. Don’t get me wrong. But in that moment with Rumi and the character, she’s so focused on the honmoon and getting that achievement that she kind of ignores her flaws. Even in the movie, you see her saying “no more hiding” while she’s hiding. That’s a really important part of that song. So as much as it’s great to strive and be ambitious and go, always do it in a healthy way, always keep talking to yourself while you’re doing it. Don’t ignore your health. Don’t ignore your mental health. A great balance, I think, is important.
paragraphs.zh: 这确实可能会这样，对吧？我发誓。就像《Golden》一样。《Golden》是一首苦中带甜的歌。严格说，它听起来是充满希望的。它确实是的。别误会。不过在那一幕里，和Rumi与角色在一起时，她过于专注于honmoon和那项成就，反而有点忽视了自己的缺点。甚至在电影里，你还能看到她说“再也不躲了”时却依然在躲着。这是这首歌里一个非常重要的部分。所以，虽然拼搏、努力、往前走都很好，但一定要用健康的方式去做，而且在做的过程中始终和自己对话。不要忽视你的身体健康。不要忽视你的心理健康。我认为，保持良好的平衡很重要。

paragraphs.en: Are you able to balance that, though? You did say you’ve been up since 5 am.
paragraphs.zh: 你真的能平衡好吗？你不是说你从早上五点就起了吗？

paragraphs.en: You got me there. I’m sometimes more ambitious; sometimes I let it go. I think it’s important to be flexible.
paragraphs.zh: 你抓到我了。我有时会更有抱负，有时又会顺其自然。我觉得重要的是要有弹性。

paragraphs.en: What would mean more to you? A Grammy or an Oscar?
paragraphs.zh: 对你来说哪一个更有意义？是格莱美奖还是奥斯卡？

paragraphs.en: Absolutely. It’s just like the team works so hard, and the soundtrack and the movie just works like a synergy in itself. It has to be both.
paragraphs.zh: 当然。就像团队非常努力一样，配乐和电影本身形成了天然的协同效应。这两者都必须同时到位。

paragraphs.en: That’s pretty ambitious.
paragraphs.zh: 那确实是非常有野心。

paragraphs.en: It is. It’s a dream.
paragraphs.zh: 是的，那是一个梦想。

paragraphs.en: Updated 10/30/25 11:45pm ET. Let us know what you think about this article. Submit a letter to the editor at [email protected].
paragraphs.zh: 更新于2025年10月30日 11:45 PM ET。请告诉我们您对这篇文章的看法。向编辑部发送来信：[email protected]。

paragraphs.en: Styling by Jacquie Trevizo. Styling assistance by Lindsey Trounson. Set design by Marie-Yan Morvan. Hair by Mark Alan Esparza. Makeup by Kevin Cheah. Nails by Taku Okamura. Suit by Sergio Hudson. Shoes by Jimmy Choo. Jewelry by Grace Lee.
paragraphs.zh: 造型由Jacquie Trevizo负责。造型助理由Lindsey Trounson协助。布景由Marie-Yan Morvan设计。发型由Mark Alan Esparza打造。化妆由Kevin Cheah完成。美甲由Taku Okamura负责。西装由Sergio Hudson提供。鞋子由Jimmy Choo提供。珠宝由Grace Lee提供。
